data.title: Graham P/E And Number: Why Realty Income Outshines Simon Property
data.description:
Realty Income and Simon Property Group: role of interest rates

I last covered Realty Income (NYSE:O) stock on 10.24. That article was an earnings preview for its FQ3 2025 and rated the stock as a buy. As for Simon Property Group (NYSE:SPG) stock, I last covered it on 6.7 with a hold rating. The rating was based on my analysis of the U.S. credit rating downgrade by Moody's at that time. Since then, a few new developments have emerged both in terms of macroeconomic parameters and also company-specific updates. The rest of this follow-up article will elaborate on the top one development in each category. For macroeconomic parameters, I will focus on the impact of the latest interest cut announced by the FOMC. For company specifics, I will focus on the actual FQ3 earnings reports (ER) released by both companies.

Let me start with interest rates. A key insight I've learned from Benjamin Graham was that stock valuation should be anchored by bond yields. I feel the insight is particularly relevant for REIT stocks due to their reliance on debt financing and also timely given the Federal Reserve's announcement for another interest rate cut by 25 basis points on Wednesday. On a more quantitative basis, in his classic, The Intelligent Investor, Graham suggested the following guideline:

Our basic recommendation is that the stock portfolio, when acquired, should have an overall earnings/price ratio-the reverse of the P/E ratio-at least as high as the current high-grade bond rate. This would mean a P/E ratio no higher than 13.3 against an AA bond yield of 7.5%.

As of Dec 1, Moody's seasoned AAA bond rates hover around 5.26%, as you can see from the following chart. Following Graham's above wisdom, such AAA bond rates could support a P/E ratio (of P/FFO ratio for REIT stocks) of around 19x. Factor in the cut in Fed fund rates, and I anticipate AAA bond rates to decrease in tandem by 25 to 50 basis points, which could potentially support a P/FFO ratio of around 20x for established REIT stocks like O and SPG.

And as detailed next, both stocks are currently trading well below this level.

O and SPG: FFO outlook

The two charts provided below show the current FFO estimates (Funds From Operations) for Realty Income and Simon Property Group in the next five years. As seen, Realty is projected to have FFO growth from $4.27 in FY 2025 to $4.97 by 2029, representing an average CAGR (Compound Annual Growth Rate) of 3.1%. With these estimates, the stock is currently priced at 13.27x P/FFO ratio only. In comparison, Simon Property is expected to see its FFO per share increase at a slightly lower rate. The FFO is projected to grow from $12.56 in 2025 to $14.06 in 2029, indicating a CAGR of 2.2% on average. With these estimates, the stock is currently priced at a 14.47x P/FFO ratio.

As such, both O and SPG are currently trading at attractive valuations when benchmarked by AAA bond rates, which motivates a closer look at their FFO growth outlook and financials. After a close examination, my assessment is that O offers a better-rounded package than SPG for reasons detailed next.

To start, I see good reasons for O's faster growth projection than SPG judging by the updates provided in their Q3 ER. O has been active on the acquisition front lately. During the first half of 2025, Realty Income acquired 173 properties (including assets under development), comprising 11.7 million square feet, with a total price tag of $2.11 billion. To better contextualize things, in the like-2024 period, O's purchases totaled $974 million. And for the full-year 2025, I anticipate a total of $4 billion in investment volume. Its balance sheet remains in sound shape despite some increases in total debt, which can support the 2025 target volume with no problem in my view.

There are some encouraging growth drivers for SPG too. Judging by its reports in the recent 2~3 quarters, I see an improving trend of occupancy rates. I expect the rates to remain at a healthy rate of 96.0% or better for the full 2025 FY. In the meantime, I also expect the base minimum rent to increase. But, on the other hand, retailer sales per square foot, an indicator of consumer spending, were a bit concerning. Looking ahead, I see good odds of subdued consumer spending to keep pressuring the fundamentals for its mall operators in the near term. Also, its balance sheet is more stretched than O, a topic I will revisit in a minute.

O and SPG: Graham P/E and Graham Number

With the above FFO growth outlook, we can perform a more careful comparison of their financials and valuation. Given the defensive nature of both stocks, I will follow the approach developed by Graham, starting with the Graham P/E defined below. Finally, as also mentioned earlier,

As a value investor to the core, Graham recommended a series of methods for investors to gauge the price they should pay. Here we will examine two of them (the other methods he recommended essentially paint the same picture). First, he recommended the PE for a defensive stock should be around 8.5 plus twice the expected annual growth rate, which I call the Graham PE. Hence, in his mind, a business that completely stagnates should be worth about 8.5x PE.

Per the consensus estimates above, I will assume an FFO growth rate of 3.1% for O stock and an AFFO growth rate of 2.2%. Based on these growth rates, the Graham P/FFO would be 14.7x for O (which equals 8.5 + 2 * 3.1). In comparison, the actual P/FFO is about 13.74x under current market conditions. As such, its current market valuation is 7% discounted from the Graham ratio. For SPG, its Graham P/FFO would be 12.9x (which equals 8.5 + 2 * 2.2). Compared to its market P/FFO ratio of 14.41x currently, the stock is trading at a 12% premium over the Graham ratio.

Graham is very aware of the margin of uncertainty in the above P/FFO (or P/E) ratios. Sources of uncertainties include the projection of future growth rates. Also, the above P/FFO ratios ignored other aspects of their financials, such as the equity, a key part of REIT stock valuation. Hence, his book also described the use of the so-called Graham Number:

In general, Graham cautions against paying a price of more than 15x times earnings or more than 1.5x times the book value ("BV"). However, a P/E multiple above 15x could be justified if the P/BV ratio is lower than 1.5x. And vice versa. And as a result, the Graham number considers both the 15x P/E limit and the 1.5x P/BV limit. More specifically, the Graham number is the square root of A) 22.5 (which equals 15 times 1.5), B) the EPS, and C) the book value.

Following the above method, my estimates of the Graham numbers for O and SPG are summarized in the above table too. The inputs I assumed include the consensus FFO estimates as mentioned earlier and also my projection of their FWD book value per share. With these inputs, the end results show the Graham number for O to be $63.3 and $51.0 for SPG. As such, O's current market price is about 10% discounted from the Graham number, while SPG's market price is more than 355% above its Graham number.

Other risks and final thoughts

Before closing, let me point out a few other risks and some limitations of my above analysis. To revisit a point I mentioned earlier, SPG's balance sheet is more stretched than O's, a risk that could limit SPG's growth capital allocation in the future. To wit, the table below compares the balance sheet data as of their Q3 ERs. As seen, Simon Property has notably higher leverage ratios. Its total debt-to-equity ratio is at 884.86%, compared to Realty's much lower ratio of 73.98%. Similarly, SPG's debt to total capital stands at 87.47%, also far higher than O's 38.5%.

To offset the issue, Simon generates ample cash flow to service its debt. The interest cut mentioned upfront could further lower borrowing costs going forward. This, of course, could benefit both companies. I just anticipate it to benefit SPG more than O, given SPG's heavier reliance than O. Furthermore, my above comparison of the Graham number overlooked an important factor: the profitability differences between O and SPG. The chart below provides a comparison between their Return on Equity (ROE) and also Return on Assets (ROA). As seen, Simon significantly outpaces Realty on both metrics. As such, it's totally reasonable to argue that every $1 on Simon's book is worth more than the same dollar on O's, this is a difference that the Graham number ignored.

To close, this reexamination of SPG and O was triggered mainly by the change in interest rates and also their updated financials announced in their FQ3 ERs. I expect the change in interest rates to better support the valuation of both companies, which are currently compressed when benchmarked by AAA bond rates following Grahams' wisdom. A closer examination shows O to be an even stronger REIT investment candidate than SPG, featuring larger valuation discounts, better growth outlook, and a more conservative balance sheet.

data.title: Repricing The World, Reframing The Portfolio: Investors Look Towards Developed International Equities With Renewed Vigor
data.description:
By Christopher Gannatti, CFA

Through the first nine months of 2025, developed-market ex-U.S. equity ETFs have attracted $76.3 billion in net inflows, a number that commands attention not just for its size, but for its trajectory. Roughly $31 billion of that arrived in the last three months alone, meaning more than 40% of the year's total came during Q3. Momentum has clearly shifted from episodic curiosity to sustained reallocation. September alone added another $9 billion, underscoring how global allocators are now leaning into regions that once sat dormant in portfolios.1

The pace and sequencing matter. In a world still digesting a higher-for-longer rate regime, this concentration of inflows signals a structural re-rating of opportunity outside the U.S. Investors are responding not to headline gross domestic product (GDP) surprises but to earnings resilience, fiscal pragmatism and corporate reform taking root across Europe and Japan.2 The flows suggest allocators are no longer viewing international diversification as a hedge. Instead, it's starting to look like a core engine of returns.

Re-Engaging with Global Equities through a Disciplined Lens

For investors looking to reweight toward developed markets outside the U.S., the WisdomTree Dynamic International Equity Fund (DDWM)3 offers an efficient bridge between macroeconomic awareness and portfolio practicality. It provides diversified exposure to dividend-paying companies across the industrialized world. It covers Europe, Japan and other developed economies, excluding the U.S. and Canada, while dynamically managing currency risk, a factor that has historically amplified volatility for U.S. dollar-based investors.

Figure 1: Standardized Returns

Performance: Consistency across Cycles, Not Just a Single Year

When evaluating any broad-based international equity strategy, the natural benchmark is the MSCI EAFE Index, the default measure of developed markets outside the U.S. and Canada. Against that backdrop, DDWM's track record speaks to consistency rather than coincidence.

Through late 2025, DDWM has delivered competitive year-to-date results, roughly in line with the MSCI EAFE. The more telling story emerges over longer horizons. Over one-, three-, five-year and longest common period comparisons, DDWM's rules-based framework has translated into higher compounded returns.

Figure 2: Consistent Performance across Cycles

Consistency as a Competitive Edge

Over the past five years, the DDWM has delivered something investors chronically underestimate: consistency through turbulence. The chart tells a clear story. While global markets oscillated through inflation spikes, tightening cycles and currency shocks, DDWM's performance line rarely lost its composure.

The outcome is notable. By November 2025, DDWM's five-year annualized return sits meaningfully above MSCI EAFE's. But more importantly, the path to those returns has been smoother. The strategy didn't depend on one heroic rally; it benefited from a disciplined process that adapts when currencies swing, rewards cash flow resilience and maintains exposure where the earnings power is most durable. In an environment where global leadership is rotating and volatility remains part of the landscape, consistency is not defensive, it's a competitive advantage.

Figure 3: A Comparison of Rolling 5-Year Annualized Returns

How Dynamic Currency Hedging Has Worked

For U.S.-based investors, foreign equity exposure carries a second layer of volatility through currency. When the U.S. dollar strengthens, returns on international equities often compress once translated back into dollars. When the dollar weakens, those same foreign returns can receive a tailwind. The challenge is that few investors can, or should, try to time those cycles.

That's where DDWM's dynamic currency hedging framework becomes an additional performance lever. The chart illustrates this: the solid line represents the Bloomberg Dollar Index, while the dashed line shows DDWM's hedge ratio, or the percentage of foreign currency exposure that's neutralized. When the dollar trend turns higher, signaling potential headwinds for unhedged international assets, the model has systematically increased the hedge ratio. Conversely, when the Bloomberg Dollar Index weakened, the model allowed more foreign-currency participation by lowering the dynamic hedge ratio.

For investors translating global opportunities back into U.S. dollars, this adaptive layer effectively reduced one of the least rewarded risks in international investing—currency swings—without requiring active intervention. Over time, it's a subtle but powerful mechanism that can have the potential to smooth the path of returns and improve consistency across market regimes.

Figure 4: How Dynamic Currency Hedging Adjusted with the Dollar Cycle

Dividends, Discipline and the Valuation Edge

At the heart of DDWM's methodology is a deceptively simple idea: start with companies that pay dividends, then weight them by the size of those dividends rather than by market capitalization. That single design choice changes the character of the portfolio. It naturally tilts DDWM toward firms with durable cash flows, shareholder-friendly balance sheets and valuation awareness, attributes that often get diluted in cap-weighted benchmarks like MSCI EAFE.

The impact shows up in the numbers. As figure 5a illustrates, DDWM's total shareholder yield of roughly 4.3%, a blend of 3.2% dividend yield and 1.1% share-reduction yield, sits above MSCI EAFE's 3.9% total. That higher yield is more than just income; it's a signal of capital discipline and a margin of safety in a world where cash flow consistency is rare.

Those same structural choices also deliver valuation resilience. By emphasizing dividend weight rather than following market capitalization, DDWM maintained lower multiples across many major metrics shown in figure 5b: price-to-earnings, price-to-cash flow, price-to-book and price-to-sales. In other words, the strategy doesn't just chase yield; it embeds valuation discipline directly into its DNA, offering investors a way to participate in global equity growth while managing one of the most persistent portfolio risks, overpaying for it.

Figure 5a: How Dividend Weighting Translated into Greater Total Shareholder Yield

Figure 5b: An Illustration of Valuation Discipline

Conclusion: Repricing the World, Reframing the Portfolio

The global market landscape of late 2025 looks fundamentally different from the one investors faced even a year ago. Capital is rotating toward developed markets beyond the U.S., and that rotation is increasingly being led by fundamentals rather than fear. In this environment, DDWM stands out as a purpose-built framework for capturing those evolving opportunities, not by prediction, but by process. Its combination of dividend-weighted stock selection, dynamic currency hedging and valuation discipline has proven capable of delivering consistent, risk-aware returns across multiple market regimes.

For investors seeking to modernize their global equity exposure, the message is clear: geographic diversification is no longer defensive; it's opportunistic. DDWM offers a structured way to participate in that shift, aligning with regions undergoing reform and reinvestment, while controlling for the volatility that often keeps U.S. investors on the sidelines. As the world continues to reprice, portfolios that can adapt systematically, the way DDWM does, may be positioned not just to keep pace, but to compound ahead.

Footnotes

1 Source: "SPDR ETFs Monthly Chart Pack," State Street Global Advisors, October 2025.

2 Source: State Street Global Advisors, 2025.

3 DDWM is designed to track the total return performance, before fees and expenses, of the WisdomTree Dynamic International Equity Index.

Important Risks Related to this Article

You cannot invest directly in an index.

Prior to April 30, 2025, the Fund was known as the WisdomTree Dynamic Currency Hedged International Equity Fund.

There are risks associated with investing, including the possible loss of principal. Foreign investing involves special risks, such as risk of loss from currency fluctuation or political or economic uncertainty. The Fund invests in derivatives in seeking to obtain a dynamic currency hedge exposure. Derivative investments can be volatile, and these investments may be less liquid than other securities and more sensitive to the effects of varied economic conditions. Derivatives used by the Fund may not perform as intended. A Fund that has exposure to one or more sectors may be more vulnerable to any single economic or regulatory development. This may result in greater share price volatility. The composition of the Index underlying the Fund is heavily dependent on quantitative models and data from one or more third parties, and the Index may not perform as intended. The Fund invests in the securities included in, or representative of, its Index regardless of their investment merit, and the Fund does not attempt to outperform its Index or take defensive positions in declining markets. Please read the Fund’s prospectus for specific details regarding the Fund’s risk profile.

Christopher Gannatti, CFA, Global Head of Research

Christopher Gannatti began at WisdomTree as a Research Analyst in December 2010, working directly with Jeremy Schwartz, CFA®, Director of Research. In January of 2014, he was promoted to Associate Director of Research where he was responsible to lead different groups of analysts and strategists within the broader Research team at WisdomTree. In February of 2018, Christopher was promoted to Head of Research, Europe, where he was based out of WisdomTree’s London office and was responsible for the full WisdomTree research effort within the European market, as well as supporting the UCITs platform globally. In November 2021, Christopher was promoted to Global Head of Research, now responsible for numerous communications on investment strategy globally, particularly in the thematic equity space. Christopher came to WisdomTree from Lord Abbett, where he worked for four and a half years as a Regional Consultant. He received his MBA in Quantitative Finance, Accounting, and Economics from NYU’s Stern School of Business in 2010, and he received his bachelor’s degree from Colgate University in Economics in 2006. Christopher is a holder of the Chartered Financial Analyst Designation.

data.title: JPMorgan Leveraging Unparalleled Strength To Build Future Growth
data.description:
When the biggest issue you have with a stock is how to make the valuation work, things are typically going pretty well, and such is the case today for JPMorgan (JPM). Valuation is indeed stretched now relative to banking norms, but given that this bank is in a class by itself with respect to its operating performance, arguments can be made that normal valuation rules don’t, or shouldn't, apply.

Even so, JPMorgan’s share price performance since my last update has been more “average” than exceptional, with the roughly 20% move in the share price since my last update on par for the large bank group as a whole. Over that time, a few stocks like Citi (C), Goldman Sachs (GS), and State Street (STT) have managed even better performances.

Apart from valuation and a little concern about the broader economy, there’s little that concerns me here now. CEO Jamie Dimon will retire at some point, but in the meantime the company has the luxury to reinvest in the business and further widen its performance gap to its peers—the $105B or so that JPMorgan plans to spend next year exceeds the market cap of U.S. Bancorp (USB), the fifth-largest bank by deposits in the United States.

Reinvesting To Widen And Deepen That Moat

Already the largest holder of deposits in the U.S., as well as one of the top players in commercial lending, trading, security services, payments, and asset management, JPMorgan is not content to rest on its laurels and instead continues to reinvest in the growth of the business.

At a recent sell-side conference, the company announced that it was going to spend even more than expected on opex in 2025 – rising opex spending by 9% (about 5% above sell-side estimates), and well ahead of the 1% to 4% spending growth expected for most large U.S. banks in 2026.

While some of this expense growth is tied to structural inflation (everything just costs more) and some to strong current volumes (driving more compensation expense and supporting costs), the company also continues to invest heavily in growing the business – whether that means new branches, new products/services, or underlying core tech investments.

To that end, JPMorgan continues to maintain a massive advantage in its IT spending – while JPMorgan actually spends a little less than average in terms of IT spend as a percentage of revenue (closer to 10% than 12%), the $18B it spends dwarfs most of the industry, with Bank of America (BAC) a distant second at around $13B, Citi at about $12B, and Wells Fargo (WFC) at $11B.

Although it’s true that spending can be wasteful and spending money doesn’t automatically guarantee success, the results do speak for themselves with JPMorgan. Over the past decade, opex spending has grown at an annualized rate of 5% here, while tangible book value per share has increased more than 120%.

Multiple Avenues To Growth

There is no shortage of potential destinations for that spending, and not just in 2025.

Already the largest deposit holder in the U.S., JPMorgan wants to grow its retail deposit share from around 11% to 15%, while also increasing its share in credit cards and connected commerce spending. To help facilitate this, management is planning to add more than 100 branches a year through 2027 while also reinvesting in service quality and product expansion.

While many large banks struggle with Net Promoter Scores (a measure of customer satisfaction), JPMorgan’s score of 44 in the 2024 J.D. Power is easily the best of the big banks, with only Regions (RF) and Citi really in the same neighborhood. This at least partly explains why JPMorgan continues to take share from banks like Bank of America, U.S. Bancorp, Truist, and Wells Fargo across many major markets, and further investments aimed at improving the customer experience should only help.

On the commercial side, the bank is looking to cross-selling as a key opportunity. While the bank is a leading player in investment banking, banking, securities services, and treasury services (including payments), a lot of those businesses have been relatively siloed in the past. Increased IT investments to streamline and simplify service integration should drive improvement here, and the incremental returns on signing up existing customers for existing services that they’re just not currently using is quite high.

There are also long-term opportunities in the asset management and private banking sides of the business. Asset management remains a very fragmented market, as JPMorgan’s 4% share is still good enough for the #5 position, and this is a lucrative cross-selling opportunity for both the consumer and commercial businesses. Private banking is likely to be a more challenging business to grow (currently around 9% share and the #5 position) given the high-touch service requirements, but here again JPMorgan’s huge breadth of services and ability to extend many of those services internationally is a significant asset.

One avenue of growth that seems likely to go largely untapped is M&A. JPMorgan’s sheer size makes acquiring another U.S. bank very challenging from a regulatory perspective, and outside of deposit-gathering businesses, the bank has generally done fine with organic growth efforts (and the failures of the Frank acquisition certainly left a bad taste).

I could see some small fintech deals and perhaps some deals meant to build up asset management, but the real question is whether JPMorgan will look to M&A outside the United States. The bank launched a digital banking business in the U.K. in 2021 and is looking to launch one in Germany next year, but it is at least plausible that the bank could look for a deal. I don’t expect this, particularly given the bank’s past successes in building businesses, but I don’t completely rule it out.

The Outlook

JPMorgan’s third quarter was a little more mixed than the prior quarters in 2025. Revenue was about 3% higher than expected (up 9% yoy and 3% qoq), but net interest income was only up 2% yoy and 3% qoq and missed by a bit. Opex grew less than 8% and beat expectations, and JPMorgan’s 51.5% efficiency ratio was easily the best of its peer group. Pre-provision profits were about 6% better than expected, but some analysts and investors didn’t like the extent to which this was driven by the volatile investment banking and trading operations.

Given the elevated opex spending in FY’26, pre-provision profit growth may only be around 1% to 2% in 2026 ahead of some re-acceleration in FY’27 (around 5% or so). That will make JPMorgan one of the slowest-growing large banks, and it will likewise compress ROTCE expansion some. The Street by and large doesn’t like this, even if it does bear fruit in terms of higher long-term growth rates and ROTCEs down the road.

I’m now looking for around core growth of 3% over the next five years and 4% over the longer term, with significant capital returns to shareholders along the way (in both buybacks and dividends). Discounting those core earnings back, I get a fair value above $300, but not much above. Likewise, premium P/TBV and P/E multiples of 3x and 14x, respectively, get me to around $300 to $320 for fair value.

The Bottom Line

I wouldn’t buy the shares of a poorly run bank just because it looked cheap, and I likewise won’t sell the shares of likely the best-run bank in the world just because they look more or less fairly valued today. Paying fair value for a top-tier asset often works out over the long term, and I’m content to remain a long-term shareholder here. That said, were the Street to start fretting again about the slower pace of growth relative to peer banks and/or were the economy to underperform, it could open another buying window for these shares.

data.title: Black Stone Minerals: A Third Production Agreement Supports An 8% Yield And Haynesville Growth (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Thesis

Black Stone Minerals (BSM) reported a new production agreement with private equity firm Caturus Energy in the company's Shelby Trough acreage. This deal represents the third Haynesville-based production agreement the company has constructed in addition to the previous agreements with Aethon and Revenant Energy.

Caturus represents a valuable third leg to the company's cash flow generation strategy out of the Haynesville. This deal structures multi-year production growth by phasing in drilling requirements over time.

However, the Caturus deal is unique in the fact that this deal is a first with a vertically integrated producer. Caturus Energy is in the process of developing its own LNG terminal through Commonwealth LNG. This links BSM with a customer with long-term offtake agreements, providing stability in production volumes. This should be welcome news for investors who remember the Aethon time out in 2024.

I am upgrading BSM to a BUY on a long term production and distribution growth thesis.

Third Quarter Results

BSM reported notable production growth in the quarter, with total production up 5% Q-o-Q. This production growth was spread evenly across both oil and natural gas volumes, helping to offset the overall decline in commodity prices. As a result, commodity-based revenue was generally in line with Q2 results.

The third quarter benefited slightly from hedging contracts that allowed the company to report a $6.9 million proceed. Combined, the company reported a distribution coverage of 1.21x, up from 1.18x in the second quarter. This performance exceeded my expectations, as I had previously modeled for a declining production scenario.

As part of the company’s earnings press release, BSM also noted strong near-term future production expectations in both the Permian and in the Haynesville. The company now expects Aethon to turn in line 12 gross wells by early 2026. The timing of these wells is likely structured to capture strong winter demand pulls.

In addition, Coterra (CTRA) is also expected to turn in line 13 Permian wells in Q4. This is expected to be followed by another 16 wells in the first half of 2026. This large pad development should significantly bolster oil production levels throughout 2026.

Cumulatively, the progress in the third quarter production growth combined with the near-term line of sight to a multitude of both natural gas and oil wells bodes well for the upcoming quarters. This dispels most of the concerns I noted in my previous analysis on BSM. In fact, the strong performance likely accelerates my timeline for the company to achieve 1.3x distribution coverage. I had previously not expected this to occur until sometime in the second quarter of 2026.

Long Term Drivers For Distribution Growth

In addition to the near-term development activity, BSM has now developed a robust strategy to facilitate growth on its Haynesville acreage. On December 2nd, the company released the news that it had agreed to a development agreement with Caturus Energy.

This agreement marks the third such deal struck in the Shelby Trough with a private equity producer. However, this deal brings forth a unique aspect to the BSM portfolio. Caturus is also developing a 9.5 MTPA LNG terminal, creating a vertically integrated customer. This helps create a price-agnostic customer who derives profit from both the transportation of the molecules and the spread between US and European hub prices. Henry Hub pricing doesn’t matter as much as contract commitments and the differential between US and international gas prices.

For those investors who still have a few sores from the Ashton timeout in 2024, this should be a welcome diversification in customer base. However, it should be noted that Commonwealth LNG is still far from complete, with the company requesting more time to complete the project.

Turning back to the production agreements, BSM expects at least 23 wells to be drilled between the three companies in 2026. The majority of these wells will still be drilled by Aethon, as this agreement is significantly more mature than the agreements with Revenant and Caturus. These newer agreements will require eight wells to be drilled, with six wells allocated to Revenant Energy and two to Caturus.

Both agreements have a multi-year ramp feature. Revenant Energy’s agreement grows to a minimum of 25 wells annually by 2030. Over a similar period and ending in 2031, Caturus will grow to a 12 well per year minimum, for a total minimum commitment of 53 wells drilled per year by 2031.

These deals also bring a small amount of exploration into the mix. The Caturus and Revenant deals are structured to help BSM delineate and explore its vast acreage. Each company is required to drill pilot and test wells in various areas of interest. This drilling is also required in order to maintain the reduced royalty rate afforded by the production agreements.

Given the recent shift in activity outside of the core Haynesville play, these test wells could prove to be invaluable to BSM over the long run. These wells effectively create an opportunity to have operators bear the operational risks of wildcatting the expansion of both the Haynesville and Bossier shale plays.

Simultaneously, this also provides conformational production data to go along with significant seismic data the company has collected over the last several quarters. Science combined with neighboring production creates a compelling data set for future producers.

Fortunately, the initial reach outside of the core has already been somewhat quantified. As shown in the image above, multiple wells have been drilled in the initial exterior regions of the core with strong production values. Initial reports indicate that as one moves away from the core, the target intervals may be considerably deeper than what is found in traditional Haynesville locations.

This is somewhat concerning for a basin that already must combat high drilling, and completion costs due to significant depths and elevated downhole temperatures. However, the shale thickness in these deeper locations is predicted to be significantly thicker, leading to slower production declines and higher EURs.

In total, BSM expects these deals to culminate in significant production growth. The company expects to grow volumes at a 10% CAGR in line to growing production to over 50 MBOE/d by 2030. The company also expects growth out into 2035, but that is getting a bit too far into the future but could be considered additional upside.

The improved scale will not only improve revenues but also improve operating margin. G&A is the largest expense on BSM’s books. By improving the scale of production, revenues should grow at a faster rate than G&A expenses. This is largely the result of royalty companies requiring generally less employment to grow than an equivalent E&P company.

The net effect is higher distributable cash flow per dollar of generated revenue. As a result, the company expects the distribution growth rate to exceed the rate of revenue growth. Initial projects are to grow the distribution to over $2/unit.

To achieve this, the company is leaning on the efficiencies created by the previously mentioned drilling agreements. The company believes these can lead to higher capital efficiency than in years past. Accordingly, the company expects leverage to stay meaningfully lower than past expansion efforts. To continue to acquire future mineral rights, the company expects the leverage ratio to grow but to be limited to less than 0.6 times EBITDA.

Valuation

Currently, BSM trades slightly over ten times EBITDA but has an improved valuation on a price-to-FCF basis. With the company currently yielding a distribution over 8%, this a reasonable price to pay.

Further support for this statement is the company’s hedge profile. In 2026, the company has approximately 80% of current production hedged with swaps at $3.67/MCF. Further, approximately 60% of oil volumes are hedged at $64.39/barrel. These are highly supportive of future cash and provide significant protection to maintaining the distribution in the near term.

Risks

BSM has aligned itself with three different private equity firms to grow volumes through drilling agreements. Private equity investments are significantly easier to spool up when margins are high. However, these groups are usually also the first to pull capital when margins shrink.

Aethon has been somewhat established as a reliable partner after drilling with BSM for several years. However, Revenant Energy and Caturus Energy are generally newer companies. The current management teams of these companies may be very qualified; however, I have been unable to find any detailed information regarding their operations. In fact, the last time I checked, Revenant Energy’s website was blank and only had 9 employees, according to LinkedIn.

It would be more comforting to have BSM’s growth ambitions tied to more publicly established operators. However, in this case investors will have to trust BSM’s management that they have brokered deals with respectable counterparties. For now, I will assume that BSM scrutinized these arrangements while also learning a thing or two following the Aethon timeout in 2024.

Investor Key Takeaways

1. Production rose 5% in Q3, exceeding my expectations.

2. Both gas and oil volumes are expected to increase due to strong activity via Ashton and Coterra TILs.

3. The company’s growth platform is geared toward the expanding core of the Haynesville. These wells have strong inventories but require high levels of capital investment. In time operators are likely to drive down well costs, supporting future development.

4. The company believes it has solid patterns to achieve a stable 53-well program in the Haynesville by 2030. This would be more than double the current well cadence.

5. BSM trades at a reasonable, although not cheap valuation. The current yield is attractive and supported by a sizable hedge book. BSM is a clear play on future Haynesville production growth.

6. Future prospects and a compelling yield are worthy of a BUY rating.

data.title: Viomi Technology: Access To The U.S. Market To Unlock Sustainable Growth
data.description:
Introduction and Thesis Statement

I am bullish on Viomi Technology Co., Ltd (VIOT), driven by its entry into North America, introducing its MASTER MI smart under-sink water purifier. This purifier has a 9-stage RO filtration system integrated with an AI touchscreen to provide real-time monitoring of volume, water quality, and filter status. I think due to MASTER MI’s capability to remove 99% of contaminants.

Accessing the US market at a time when 64% of Americans have concerns about tap water and only 9% have no concern at all, this shows an existing gap in terms of offering a purification solution. The US water purification market is estimated to grow from $6.8 billion in 2024 to $11.0 billion in 2032. Already, the market penetration of the US water purification market is about 50%, meaning the other half is up for grabs.

Looking at the MASTER MI 9-stage RO filtration system in this US market, I think that is why it has achieved a strong market entry by being in the Top 19 in Amazon’s US water purifiers category by sales volume. Under the sink tankless RO purifier category, the company has attained position 4, which is the highest rank in the history of VIOMI on the Amazon platform. I believe that with the competitive products, which have seen an impressive market entry like in the US, the leverage that has kept its YTD price return at 57.63% beating the S&P 500’s 17.09%.

I will now dive deeper into why I think accessing the US market warrants a buy rating for Viomi Technology.

Company Brief

Viomi Technology Co., Ltd, develops and sells IoT-enabled smart home products through its subsidiaries. The company has a portfolio that includes smart water purification systems, kitchen appliances, and water heaters. The company also sells a wide range of smart devices, such as washing machines, water quality meters, interactive screen TVs, and blenders, among others. Viomi sells complementary consumables like portable fans and speakers directly and through online platforms like Tmall, JD.com, Youpin, and Pinduoduo. It was incorporated in 2014, and it is headquartered in Guangzhou, China.

The Bigger Picture

Before I get to the specifics of MASTER MI smart under-sink purifier, let’s dive into why I think market entry into the US shows potential revenue growth. I introduced that there are 64% of Americans concerned about their tap water, and only 9% have no concerns. Following this concern, 68% of Americans state that the major reason why they are concerned, and use filtration is to remove harmful contaminants, followed by taste improvement. There are other motivators, but what stands out here is that there is a concern about tap water, and this is what motivates Americans to filter their water.

At 64%, concerns about harmful contaminants explain why today 73 million Americans are exposed to Per- and Polyfluoroalkyl Substrates [PFAS]. This research also highlights that in 98% of the sites sampled, there is at least one type of PFAS after frequent tests that surpass federal drinking water levels. These kinds of exposures increase risks related to thyroid disease, cancers, immune system problems, and liver damage, among other health problems.

Owing to my explanation of the market above, I take it as a gap that needs to be filled by new and innovative water purifiers in the US market. From a global perspective, water purification is growing at a CAGR of 7.44% between 2024 and $36.5 million to $64.5 million in 2033. China will take a larger market share, from $6.2 billion in 2024 to $12.4 billion, which is understandable as its market penetration is still at an early stage of 28%. Compared to the US, which is 50% penetrated, the remaining market share is up for capture.

This is why I believe that the 2026 flagship of MASTER MI AI Alkaline under-sink water purifier will serve the US market. Given its impressive market entry in the US, this shows that Viomi Technology water purifiers have a better technological capability compared to peer brands. Now it has secured around 1,8467 patents in water purification technology and 690 invention patents.

You see, with a production capacity of 5 million water purifiers and 30 million filter elements, the company has the capacity to produce water purifiers for the North American market. The product (MASTER M1 Alkaline water purifier) is specifically targeted to solve the problem of quality water relating to PFAS contamination in North America. Already, the signs of a good market acceptance have started showing a positive side by being in the Top 19 in the Amazon US water purifier category.

Financials & Valuation

Viomi’s top-line was not doing well between 2021 and 2023, dipping from $892.4 million in 2020 to a low of $230.9 million in 2023. This was primarily due to sluggish demand in China, which affected home appliances and water purification systems. However, China continues to boost its consumer spending, and I believe this is also contributing to a revenue rebound in Viomi. For instance, the trailing revenue is currently at $385.3 million, representing a 23.81% YoY. The good thing about Viomi is that, according to the consensus revenue estimate for FY2025, the revenues might hit $415.07 million, representing 42.15% YoY growth. As China's buying power continues to increase, and considering the entry into the US market is still in early stages, the revenues in 2026 have a modest increase to $419.07 million, representing 0.96% YoY growth. Nevertheless, I expect this to improve in 2027 and beyond as the company keeps expanding in the US market. As such, I believe a growth rate averaging 15% is likely alongside the projected US Consumer Discretionary sector growth of about 14%.

Moving to profitability, VIOT is revealing to have a strong execution with a trailing net income of $24.8 million up from $8.7 million in 2024 representing more than 180% growth. Viomi’s debut on Amazon during Black Friday/Cyber Monday, which saw Viomi products achieve Top 19 in the water purifier category, is a sign of good acceptance. Owing to this optimism, I think this is a major reason behind the consensus EPS estimates of $0.45 in FY2025, representing 254.81% and is likely to maintain the same $0.45 as it approaches new markets such as the US market. The interplay here is the expected high revenue growth against what appears to be a relatively stable operating expenses.

As I conclude the section, I will value VIOT using the P/S multiple comparatively to that of its competitors, Haier Smart Home Co., Ltd (HRSHF) and Panasonic Holdings Corporation (PCRFF). The P/S multiple of VIOT is 0.41x, and compared to its peers, HRSHF at 0.81x and PCRFF at 0.56x, it indicates that the market has undervalued VIOT.

When I look at the trailing growth rates, I see VIOT as a value opportunity. This is because with a trailing YoY revenue growth of 23.81%, none of these peers is close to it. The nearest is HRSHF at 6.55% and PCRFF lags at -6.97%, both averaging -0.21% despite the average P/S of 0.69x being 1.7x that of VIOT. This is a clear undervaluation which I believe the market should seize. While I expect this company to sustain a growth rate in the north of 10% past 2026 due to its new market expansion, I expect a market revaluation. Assuming a target P/S of 0.7x which is the average for the peers, and the consensus revenue of $415.07 for 2025, I estimate a price target of $4.28.

Investment Risks

High SG&A Burn Rate Tied To US Expansion

The Selling General and Administration costs are trailing at $48.3 million, up from $38.6 million in 2024. This means that although the company is having a good market acceptance, it is at an increasing cost. This indicates the company is spending more on logistics and marketing in the US entry. In case the sales fail to pick up as intended, this may affect scale proportionally, eroding margins.

Exposure to U.S.-China Trade Tension

The US-China trade tensions are still ongoing, and China’s manufacturing and supply chain is likely to affect water purifiers. Material risk is one of the areas facing regulatory barriers. This could raise the cost and price competitiveness of its water purifiers in the American market, which could reduce the revenues. With initiatives such as Black Friday recording success, it also reveals there is an existing demand, but with the prolonged geopolitical friction, this may erode the margins or disrupt logistics and financial forecasts.

Conclusion

I believe that early market acceptance in the US market, at a time it is facing a purified water challenge, has an upside reversal pattern. This is why I am reiterating my bullish stance, hence my buy rating.

data.title: FNDB: Turning Toward More Value In 2026
data.description:
Barring some unforeseen collapse in the market in the final weeks of the year, 2025 has been another banner year for growth stocks. This is nothing new for investors who have only been in the market for a decade or so. US growth has outperformed US value investing eight out of the last nine years. With a strong push at the end of December, the S&P 500 could notch its third straight year of 20%+ returns. The last time that happened was at the end of the 1900s, and the index followed with three straight years of losses in 2000, 2001, and 2002.

The Shiller PE, which measures a cyclically adjusted valuation for the S&P 500, is at its second highest level of all time. The only time that the metric has been greater than it is today was in the year 2000, right before the index rattled off those three straight down years. While, past results are no guarantees for the future, it does seem reasonable that investors who have been heavily invested in large- and mega-cap growth stocks may want to recheck the balance of their investments and consider exposure to stocks who offer a little more in terms of traditional value.

The Schwab Fundamental U.S. Broad Market ETF (FNDB) may be just the right option for this broadening of exposure to more value-oriented stocks. The fund does not leave growth behind completely, but it balances out a smaller allocation to some of the more popular large-cap tech stocks with stocks from other sectors that may perform better during a slowdown in the economy. With a track record that goes back over a decade, the fund has lived through some down years and bounced back, with its performance over the last five years being within striking distance of the S&P 500. I consider FNDB to be an ETF worth buying heading into what could be a more volatile new year.

ETF Overview

The Schwab Fundamental U.S. Broad Market ETF has the stated objective of tracking the total return of an index that measures “The performance of U.S.-based companies based on their fundamental size and weight.” That index is the little-known RAFI Fundamental High Liquidity US All Index, which evaluates their selections by fundamentals and includes stocks with high liquidity that are easier to buy and sell. These fundamental measures may include variables such as operating cash flow, sales, and dividends. FNDB was started in August of 2013, and the fund is passively managed.

FNDB has just over $1 billion in assets under management as of December 12. The top 10 holdings in the fund are listed below. Even though the fund leans toward more value characteristics, investors can still see four of the Mag Seven companies on this top 10 list. Apple (AAPL) sits at the top with a portfolio weight of 4.29%. Microsoft (MSFT) is second at 2.45%. Alphabet Class A (GOOGL) shares are third and the only other stock that holds more than 2% of the fund.

Meta Platforms (META), NVIDIA (NVDA), and Tesla (TSLA) are noticeably missing from the list, although they are included in the fund’s holdings. META actually just missed the cut for the top 10 with a weight of 1.18%. NVDA is much farther down the list with a 0.37% share of the ETF’s assets. TSLA has an allocation of just 0.33%. FNDB currently lists 1630 total holdings on its online profile.

Additional details about the fund’s holdings can be seen in the tables below. The sector allocations for FNDB are much more balanced than a typical index fund. Technology stocks make up just under 17% of this fund. They represent almost twice that amount in Schwab’s Russell 1000 Index ETF (SCHK). Financials hold almost the same allocation in FNDB with a weight of 16.3%. The investments that would have been assigned to the tech sector are spread out through several others, like Energy and Consumer Staples. Those two sectors have a combined allocation of 7.6% in SCHK. They total 15.6% together in FNDB.

The table above also shows that FNDB provides access to investments in both mid-cap and small-cap stocks. Large-cap stocks make up about 59.5% of the fund’s portfolio. Mid-caps control about a quarter of the fund. The allocation to small-cap stocks is just above 15%.

FNDB has a current dividend yield of 1.6%. Payouts are made quarterly, with the final distribution from 2025 hitting shareholder accounts on December 15. While dividends are not a significant reason to own the fund, it has seen its payout increase for four straight years.

Analysis of FNDB

One of the first things I want to check with any ETF is its past performance, and FNDB certainly has an extended history of positive results. Since 2016, the fund has been in the top half of all other ETFs in its category nine out of 10 years, with its only underperformance coming in 2022. Morningstar says the fund is in the top 10% of 916 similar funds over the last five years and the top 6% of 761 funds over the last decade. For its risk-adjusted returns, the FNDB has a five-star rating.

To compare FNDB to a pure value fund is a little bit misleading only because the fund does have some exposure to growth stocks as well. Schwab does manage a large-cap value ETF (SCHV) that is considered part of the same investment class, but its portfolio looks quite different. SCHV does not have any Mag Seven stocks in its top 10 holdings, or in its entire portfolio, for that matter.

However, even with these growth stocks in the fold, the Schwab Fundamental U.S. Broad Market ETF still holds a collection of stocks that trade at a significant discount to the broader market. From the top 10 holdings, the difference between FNDB and the S&P 500 is clear. The chart below shows just how much the fund differs from a slightly more inclusive index fund like Schwab’s Russell 1000 Index ETF.

While there is a slight advantage in earnings growth in the Russell 1000 index fund, the valuation gaps between these two ETFs appear much more significant. Investors in FNDB pay over $5.50 less for a dollar of earnings and own a collection of stocks that trade for almost half of the book value of the average stock in SCHK. The price-to-sales ratio for FNDB is over 60% less than the same metric in SCHK.

The expense ratio for FNDB is 0.25%, which is higher than I expected and more than I generally see in a passively managed index fund. As a point of comparison, SCHK has an expense ratio of just 0.03%. The slight elevation in FNDB's cost is really the only detraction I could find for giving the fund a Buy rating. However, the fund's track record of above-average returns for its category as well as its unique approach to selecting stocks that may be better suited for the year ahead outweigh this concern.

Risks to Consider

While the Schwab Fundamental U.S. Broad Market ETF does have a tilt toward value, it is still almost completely exposed to the US stock market. A downturn in economic activity in 2026, an increase in inflation, and higher unemployment could all lead to losses for the fund. The odds of a recession in 2026 vary widely depending on the source of the prediction, with some putting the odds as high as 50%. During its worst three-month period, in the first quarter of 2020, FNDB lost 26.8% of its value, so the fund is not totally immune to large losses in a short period of time.

On the flip side, the fund may also underperform next year if growth stocks take the spotlight once again. Over the last year, FNDB has underperformed both the S&P 500 as well as the Schwab Russell 1000 Index ETF, but the margin of difference in total return is surprisingly small. The gap is much more noticeable over the last three years, when large-cap tech names have experienced exceptional success. Interestingly, if you include the turmoil from 2022 and look back over a five-year period, the difference is significantly less, and FNDB actually outperforms SCHK.

The Bottom Line

Timing the market is always an inexact science, and there is a possibility that the bull market in growth stocks will continue well into next year. However, the results from the table above give me some comfort in the merit of owning FNDB over a longer period of time. The fund is almost taking a "Goldilocks" approach to investing, where the portfolio has some exposure to growth while still having a definite slant toward a broader selection of companies with lower valuations.

FNDB has a solid track record of success and a stock selection method that has been tested during periods of growth and contraction. I think that this ETF is one worthy of strong consideration for addition to a balanced equity portfolio for 2026.

data.title: U.S. IPO Weekly Recap: Wealthfront Leads 4 IPO Week, While United Rentals Challenger Submits Filings
data.description:
Four IPOs and six SPACs debuted this week. Eight IPOs and four SPACs submitted filings.

Digital investing platform Wealthfront (WLTH) priced at the top of the range to raise $485 million at a $2.6 billion market cap. Known as a “robo-advisor,” Wealthfront’s platform offers a suite of financial products that span a broad risk spectrum delivered through web and mobile channels, such as cash management, investment advisory, borrowing and lending, and financial planning. The company targets younger “digital-first” clients, including Millennials and Gen Z. As of July 31, 2025, it had over 1.3 million funded clients and $88.2 billion in platform assets. Wealthfront boasts an above-average margin profile due to its low-cost model, although its top-line growth has been slowing. It finished the week up 1%.

Outpatient radiology network Lumexa (LMRI) priced at the midpoint to raise $463 million at a $1.8 billion market cap. The company is a national provider of outpatient diagnostic imaging services, operating 184 centers across 13 states through a mix of wholly owned sites and joint ventures with health systems. The company offers a range of advanced and routing imaging modalities, spanning MRIs, CTs, PET scans, X-rays, ultrasounds, and mammograms. Formed in 2018 by Charlotte Radiology and investor WCAS under the name US Radiology Specialists, Lumexa has made 20 acquisitions, giving it a leading position in a fragmented market, although it is levered at 3.7x pro forma debt/LTM EBITDA. It finished the week flat.

Construction services provider Cardinal Infrastructure (CDNL) priced at the midpoint to raise $242 million at a $769 million market cap. Cardinal provides wet utility installations, like water, sewer, and stormwater systems, as well as grading, paving, and other site development work for residential, commercial, industrial, municipal, and state projects. The company's business strategy is focused on the Southeastern US, and it primarily operates in the greater Charlotte, Raleigh, and Greensboro areas of North Carolina. As of December 31, 2024, Cardinal was in various stages of completion on approximately 100 projects. Its growing backlog was worth approximately $646 million as of September 30, 2025, although its margins have recently contracted slightly. Cardinal finished the week up 41%.

Hong Kong-based merchandise sourcing provider JM Group (JMG) priced its US IPO at the bottom of the range to raise $15 million at a $79 million market cap. JM Group sources and wholesales a wide range of merchandise for retailers, distributors, and brand owners, spanning categories such as sports and outdoors, toys and games, and clothing, among others. It finished the week up 24%.

Six SPACs also priced this week. Meshflow Acquisition (MESHU) raised $300 million to target blockchain infrastructure and the digital asset ecosystem. Karbon Capital Partners (KBONU) raised $300 million to target energy and energy infrastructure. Daedalus Special Acquisition (DSACU) raised $225 million to target consumer-facing AI and technology. ITHAX Acquisition III (ITHAU), formed by Ithaca Capital Partners, raised $200 million. Bluerock Acquisition (BLRKU), led by executives from alternative asset manager Bluerock, raised $150 million. Twelve Seas III (TWLVU) raised $150 million to target oil and gas companies located outside of the US.

data.title: Trade Deficit Shrinks To Lowest Level Since June 2020
data.description:
Originally published on December 9, 2025

By Jennifer Nash

The U.S. Energy Information Administration (EIA) has released its latest Short-Term Energy Outlook (STEO), providing forecasts for energy markets. This article presents the annual production outlooks for crude oil, natural gas, and natural gas liquids (NGLs), comparing the December 2025 projections against the previous month's estimates. These forecasts are particularly relevant for energy infrastructure companies operating in the U.S.

The Role of Energy Infrastructure in the Economy

Energy infrastructure forms the bridge between energy production (supply) and consumption (demand). It includes the transportation, storage, and processing of crude oil, natural gas, and NGLs through a network of pipelines, terminals, and other infrastructure.

"The global economy requires vast amounts of energy. As the world’s largest producer of oil, natural gas, and NGLs, energy infrastructure plays a critical role in connecting US energy production with domestic and global demand," adds Stacy Morris, Head of Index Product Research at TMX VettaFi.

U.S. Crude Oil Production Outlook

The December 2025 STEO shows an increase in the forecast for U.S. crude oil production compared to the November 2025 outlook. For 2025, the forecast was raised by 0.1% to 13.61 million barrels per day (MMBpd), and for 2026, it was lowered by -0.3% to 13.53 MMBpd. This represents a projected yearly growth rate of 2.8% from 2024 to 2025, but a decline of -0.5% is expected from 2025 to 2026.

U.S. Natural Gas Production Outlook

The forecast for U.S.-marketed natural gas production has also seen an upward revision in the December 2025 STEO. The 2025 forecast increased by 0.1% to 118.38 billion cubic feet per day (Bcf/d), and the 2026 forecast rose by 1.2% to 120.05 Bcf/d compared to the November 2025 outlook. This represents a projected yearly growth rate of 4.6% from 2024 to 2025, and an increase of 1.4% is expected from 2025 to 2026.

U.S. Natural Gas Liquids (NGL) Production Outlook

Natural Gas Plant Liquids (NGLs) production is also expected to increase, with the December 2025 STEO showing a rise in forecasts. The 2025 NGL production forecast was raised by 0.5% to 7.43 million barrels per day (MMBpd), and the 2026 forecast saw a 0.7% increase to 7.60 MMBpd. This reflects a yearly growth rate of 5.5% from 2024 to 2025 and a further 2.3% growth from 2025 to 2026.

ETFs related to the energy infrastructure space include the Alerian MLP ETF (AMLP) and the Alerian Energy Infrastructure ETF (ENFR).

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Celsius: Stronger Ties With Pepsi (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
I rate Celsius Holdings (CELH) as a buy, as the company is reinforcing its partnership with Pepsi (PEP) gradually, so I would be more of "accumulate with caution," as there is still pending evidence to reinforce a stronger buy rating with higher conviction. Celsius has taken very important steps since my last article, such as the acquisitions of Alani Nu and Rockstar, but what gives me more confidence is the changes associated with the role of Pepsi in Celsius' distribution. Even when I think that it might cause some volatility in Celsius' stock performance in the next quarters, I am sure it's the right decision from a long-term perspective while avoiding repeating the problems related to the destocking of inventories in 2023 and 2024. Let's explore it.

Context

In Q3 2025, Celsius delivered good results in general; revenue growth was 172.9% YoY, reaching USD 725 million, beating expectations; gross margins also showed a substantial improvement from 46% to 51%, but net income was negative, -USD 61, as a result of the distribution termination fees:

This expense of USD 246 million is most likely related to the shift from Congo Brands distribution, the former owner of Alani Nu, to Pepsi's distribution, in which there would be a prior contractual agreement between Congo Brands and Alani Nu. I would expect that those distribution termination fees are a one-off.

On the other hand, adjusted diluted EPS was USD 0.42 per share, surpassing analyst consensus. Of course GAAP EPS was USD -0.27 per share because of the termination fees I explained above.

Now, 9-month revenue reached USD 1,793 million, which represents an annual growth of 75% YoY, whereas 9-month net income experienced -49.23% YoY, mostly influenced by the termination fees I previously mentioned.

Guidance for FY 2025

Celsius expects a full-year revenue of USD 3.76-3.79 billion, which would mean a strong revenue growth of 178% YoY. GAAP EPS (full year) is expected to reach between USD -1.05 and -0.75 per share and adjusted EPS (non-GAAP) between USD 6.10 and 6.40 per share.

This wide gap between GAAP EPS and non-GAAP EPS shows that management expects significant non-cash expenses, such as amortization and depreciation, and integration-related charges, such as restructuring costs mainly associated with the new Pepsi captaincy as part of a new distribution agreement with Celsius, which involves the recent Celsius acquisitions of Alani Nu and Rockstar.

Celsius acquires Alani Nu and Rockstar

There is no doubt that Alani Nu and Rockstar (US and Canada assets) will open new growth avenues for Celsius, particularly Alani Nu, given its size and huge growth (114% YoY in Q3), while holding a solid market share of 7.2% dollar share of the US ready-to-drink energy category.

In February 2025, Celsius acquired Alani Nu, which is a female-focused health & wellness beverage brand, paying USD 1.8 million, including taxes. This is a very good acquisition, as it will enable Celsius to grow in new markets.

If we take the revenues of Alani Nu and Celsius from FY2024, we would see that Alani Nu would represent around 44% of Celsius's total sales, which is a meaningful acquisition.

On the other hand, the other Celsius acquisition is the Rockstar's assets from the US and Canada. Rockstar holds a market share of 2.4% dollar share of the US ready-to-drink energy category. Rockstar experienced a decline of 9% YoY in Q3 2025, which I think is a temporary setback as Pepsi and Celsius finish their migration of systems and logistics under the new Pepsi's expanded responsibility in the distribution of Celsius products.

Indeed, these new acquisitions mean not only two new subsidiaries within Celsius but also a new distribution platform and operations under Pepsi's distribution, the new Pepsi's captaincy.

Celsius-Pepsi vs. Monster-Coke: Unequal Energy Distribution Play

This new arrangement in 2025 between Pepsi and Celsius is a material change with respect to my last article in November 2024, as I mentioned in it that there was a stronger commitment in the deal between Coca-Cola (KO) and Monster (MNST) than that of Pepsi and Celsius, so my perception then was that Pepsi's support to Celsius needed to be stronger for triggering a better Celsius performance over the long haul.

In this sense, one of the key aspects I noticed in my last article was that Coca-Cola owns Monster's common shares and Pepsi owns Celsius' preferred shares that pay annual dividends of 5%. It has important implications about how those investments are registered in Coca-Cola's and Pepsi's financial statements, respectively, which might trigger different incentives for each other to support their respective investees.

Indeed, Pepsi's investment in Celsius' preferred shares is registered as an available-for-sale asset (AFS) in Pepsi's balance sheet. Any change of the investment is registered in the accumulated other comprehensive income (AOCI), which is an account registered within the equity in the balance sheet. Pepsi's net income will not be impacted by that investment, as any loss in that investment would be included in the AOCI in the equity of Pepsi's balance sheet. Pepsi's ROE and ROIC might even be pushed up as Pepsi's equity would reduce if Celsius' preferred shares go south.

Conversely, Coca-Cola registered its investment in Monster's common shares using the equity method, which means that Monster's revenues and net income are included in Coca-Cola's income statements. In this sense, I stated in my last article that this kind of investment, through common shares, represented a way better alignment than that of Pepsi with Celsius, as Coca-Cola had the incentives that Monster performs very well to show better combined revenues and net income.

This evidence showed me that Pepsi did not consider its investment in Celsius as strategic as that of Monster with Coca-Cola when I assessed Celsius in November 2024. Nevertheless, now there are clear steps toward a stronger partnership that may support Celsius' long-term performance.

Pepsi's captaincy: a gradual solution to Celsius's destocking problem and a stronger relationship

The beginning of a stronger relationship between Pepsi and Celsius might have started as a result of the serious problems associated with the destocking of inventories in 2023 and 2024, and both partners might have agreed about a new partnership strategy during the acquisition of Rockstar, as I saw the next table in the Celsius 10Q for the Q3 FY 2025:

Indeed, in the table above, it's shown that there was an exchange of assets between Pepsi and Celsius; Celsius issued preferred shares for USD 935.7 million to Pepsi, whereas Pepsi sold Rockstar's assets to Celsius for USD 307 million in a non-cash operation.

Of those USD 935.7 million, only USD 585 million are convertible preferred shares into common shares, which would increase Pepsi's current stake from 8.5% to around 11% in Celsius if Pepsi converts them into common shares on a given future date. This is a higher commitment between Pepsi and Celsius, even if those shares are still preferred.

In this sense, under these exchanges, Celsius issued preferred shares of USD 937.7 million, and Pepsi sold the Rockstar assets from the US and Canada for USD 307 million and the working capital of USD 29 million, so there is a difference of USD 598.7 million in favor of Pepsi, which is registered as a non-cash upfront payment to Pepsi (black circle); in other words, Celsius is paying Pepsi, with preferred shares, for captaincy for distribution of the brands Celsius, Rockstar, and Alani Nu.

In my view, this is critical to understand since this is what separates the Celsius of 2025 from the Celsius before 2025.

In Celsius' 10Q, I've found the following about Pepsi's captaincy:

Under the Captaincy, Pepsi is obligated to use commercially reasonable efforts to sell and distribute the Company’s energy drink portfolio in the U.S. and to prioritize the Company's products within its beverage distribution system. The arrangement provides the Company with enhanced control and oversight of the energy drink category within Pepsi’s U.S. distribution network, including the ability to determine product facings, merchandising allocations and certain promotional priorities for energy beverages.

This is a total expansion of responsibilities for Pepsi, since it implies that Pepsi will manage the inventory planning involvement, the demand forecasting, and the retail execution through a unified portfolio management, which includes Celsius, Rockstar, and Alani Nu. In other words, Pepsi has incentive alignment, controlling the retail execution more deeply, unifying data across all retailers, and centralizing a prior fragmented distribution.

Before 2025, Pepsi's job was simply to execute delivery, not design strategy; indeed, I haven't found anything about the word "captaincy" in any of the 10Ks and 10Qs before 2025. It means that, in previous years, Celsius was mainly responsible for its retail execution strategy, product planning, SKU prioritization, and shelf management with clients.

Now, you can understand that that was the main factor that caused the destocking problem in 2024, as there was a serious misalignment between Pepsi and Celsius. Indeed, Pepsi bottlers aggressively loaded channels because Celsius was growing exponentially, bottlers were incentivized by volume, and Pepsi did not control upstream planning, only downstream sales.

As such, these changes in Pepsi's expanded responsibility, the increase of the potential stake in Pepsi to 11% of Celsius, and the acquisitions of Alani Nu and Rockstar give me more confidence about Celsius' prospects. However, there are still some challenges in the short and middle term.

Celsius will require a robust share buyback program through 2031 and flawless execution of its partnership with Pepsi

After the agreement of Pepsi's captaincy in the distribution, the automatic conversion date of Pepsi's preferred shares was extended to 2031, with that conversion happening automatically if certain conditions are met.

Now, it's important to know how many shares Celsius will need to issue in 2031 if Pepsi's preferred shares are converted into new common shares, which will dilute current shareholders. Celsius reported that 22 million common shares are required for the Series A preferred shares and 11.3 million common shares to satisfy the Series B preferred shares, so 33 million common shares will be issued in 2031 if they are automatically converted.

Assuming an average Celsius stock price of USD 50 per share, that would mean that Celsius needs to repurchase around USD 1.6 billion to mitigate dilution among its current investors. The good news is that Celsius announced on November 10, 2025, a new repurchase program of USD 300 million, preparing the path for the future conversion of the preferred shares held by Pepsi. If Celsius keeps spending USD 300 million per year until 2031, most of the potential dilution as a result of the conversion of the preferred shares held by Pepsi to common shares will be mitigated.

On the other hand, related to the changes in Pepsi's responsibilities as part of its captaincy, this implies migrations of distributions and logistics, information transfer and data integrity, and supply chain disruptions, among other restrictions. In other words, this new change in the strategy of the distribution and planification is very good over the long haul for Celsius, but it will not be an easy path, which can create short-term setbacks that might impact Celsius's performance in the upcoming quarters.

So even when I upgraded to "buy" Celsius stock, it was more a "soft buy," which implies accumulation with caution to take advantage of the potential volatility of the stock in the next quarters.

Valuation

According to SA Quant ratings, the stock seems to be expensive, as the grade is D+, being more expensive than its group of comparables. However, we need to take into account that, most likely, none of its comparables has a revenue growth like that shown by Celsius.

Let's see a valuation method that enables us to have more control of the different assumptions. I will use the following assumptions:

  • Outstanding shares: 252.8 million (shares diluted as of September 2025)

  • FCF Margins: 10% (FCF margins in 2024 and 2023 were 17% and 9.3%, respectively, but it's a very volatile metric in Celsius)

  • I assume that Celsius is held until the year 2030.

  • Revenue growth from 2025 to 2026: according to consensus.

  • Revenue growth beyond 2026: my own expectations under a conservative scenario.

  • P/FCF 2027: 50x (optimistic assumption)

  • Discount rate: 10% (given the changes in the strategy toward more predictability for the long haul)

Based on our assumptions, I projected the revenues until 2030; I was very conservative in the years 2028, 2029, and 2030, as it's supposed that Pepsi's distribution will be very well established in those years after all the changes started in 2025 as part of the new partnership agreement. I was conservative, assuming a growth of 10% in those 3 last years, reaching a revenue of USD 4.84 billion for 2030. I multiply those revenues by my assumption of 10% FCF margins to get an estimated FCF for 2030 of USD 484.6 million.

Now, I take that FCF of USD 484.6 million to be divided by the outstanding number of shares of 252.8 million to get an FCF per share for 2030, getting 1.92. In this sense, I take the FCF per share of 1.92 and multiply it by 50x, which is closer to the lowest end of a range between 38x and 129x in the last 4 years.

Then, I get a target price of USD 95.9 per share in 2030, so I calculate the present value to bring it back to 2025.

Thus, finding the intrinsic value:

Intrinsic value = 95.9/(1+discounted rate)^5

Finally, I get an approximate intrinsic value of USD 54 per share, which would leave us a potential upside of 38% considering the current stock price. Even with that upside, I take a "soft" buy or accumulate with caution, as there could still be some logistic challenges related to the migration to Pepsi's distribution in Alani Nu's case and Pepsi's expanded responsibility with the Celsius brand.

On the other hand, under a strong execution by the partnership between Pepsi and Celsius, the intrinsic value can be way higher, but I take a conservative stance as Celsius and Pepsi need to prove a smooth transition to the new strategy implemented.

As a result, there could be some volatility in Celsius performance that might cause higher volatility in Celsius' stock price. Long-term investors like to take advantage of the ups and downs of the stock price to accumulate shares gradually, reflecting the short-term adjustments in the Celsius strategy and knowing, at the same time, that there is a sound long-term strategy behind

Risks

There are two risks to my thesis: i) an outstanding execution of the partnership between Pepsi and Celsius making a very smooth transition to the new strategy, which can reduce the volatility in the inventories while strengthening Celsius' predictability, making my thesis too conservative, and ii) a very poor execution of the partnership between Pepsi and Celsius causing so much volatility in the inventories, repeating a similar problem like the destocking in 2024, combined with high integration costs, pushing down the stock price as a result of the lack of confidence by the market in a smooth transition combined with poor execution.

So, my thesis takes into account both scenarios: accumulate with caution in case things go south and you are not fully invested, and, at the same time, accumulate with caution in case the company delivers better than expected. Behind that thesis is the fact that there have been strong advances in the partnership between Celsius and Pepsi, which aligns their goals better.

In the upcoming years, and looking into the table above, I would expect better returns on capital and more consistent high revenue growth combined with consistently high FCF margins. I think that we'll see that as Celsius keeps taking rational decisions, but we expect those decisions to be well executed.

Takeaways

Celsius has strengthened its relationship with Pepsi through a new arrangement that provides Pepsi with enhanced control and oversight of Celsius' energy drink portfolio within Pepsi’s U.S. distribution network. This new arrangement, combined with Pepsi's higher participation in Celsius with a new potential of an 11% stake and the acquisitions of Alani Nu and Rockstar, changes the picture I had, which made me upgrade the stock to a "buy," or more an "accumulation with caution."

In addition, in my previous article, I mentioned that Pepsi would convert its preferred shares into common shares in 2028 if certain conditions are met, which were not disclosed at that time. Now, in 2025, there are some changes, as the option to convert them is still 2028, giving other opportunities for Pepsi to make the conversion automatically in 2031 and disclosing the conditions to be met for that conversion.

All this shows me that Pepsi is more involved in its investment in Celsius while advancing in its commitment. Although the relationship between Pepsi and Celsius is not as strong as that of Coca-Cola and Monster yet, there is a clear advancement toward that level of commitment, and that is a material change with respect to my previous article.
